title: Graphs
description: Master graph representation and traversal in Python — adjacency lists, DFS, BFS, and connected components are foundational patterns that appear in nearly every coding interview.

sections.title: Building an adjacency list
sections.explanation: An adjacency list maps each node to its list of neighbors and is the most common graph representation in interviews. Use a `defaultdict(list)` to avoid key-existence checks — just append neighbors directly. Interviewers expect you to build one from an edge list in O(V + E) and handle both directed and undirected variants.
sections.example:
from collections import defaultdict

edges = [[0, 1], [0, 2], [1, 3], [2, 3]]
graph = defaultdict(list)
for u, v in edges:
    graph[u].append(v)
    graph[v].append(u)

for node in sorted(graph):
    print(f"{node}: {sorted(graph[node])}")

exercise.prompt: Given `edges = [[0, 1], [0, 2], [1, 2], [2, 3]]`, build an undirected adjacency list using `defaultdict(list)`. Print each node and its sorted neighbor list, iterating nodes in sorted order.
exercise.starterCode:
from collections import defaultdict

edges = [[0, 1], [0, 2], [1, 2], [2, 3]]
graph = defaultdict(list)
for u, v in edges:
    graph[u].append()
    graph[v].append()

for node in sorted(graph):
    print(f"{node}: {sorted(graph[node])}")

validation.message: Each node should list its neighbors in sorted order for the undirected graph
Use defaultdict(list) and append to build the adjacency list

exercise.hint: For an undirected graph, append v to graph[u] and u to graph[v] for each edge.

sections.title: DFS on a graph
sections.explanation: Depth-first search explores as deep as possible before backtracking, using recursion and a `visited` set to avoid cycles. DFS is the backbone of path-finding, cycle detection, and topological sort problems. Interviewers expect O(V + E) time and the ability to switch between recursive and iterative (stack-based) implementations.

exercise.prompt: Implement recursive DFS starting from node 0. Use a `visited` set to track seen nodes. Print each node as you visit it (space-separated on one line). The graph is `{0: [1, 2], 1: [0, 3], 2: [0, 4], 3: [1], 4: [2]}`.

validation.message: DFS from 0 visiting sorted neighbors should produce 0 1 3 2 4
Use a visited set and recurse with dfs(graph, neighbor, visited)

exercise.hint: Check `if neighbor not in visited`, then recurse with `dfs(graph, neighbor, visited)`. Sorting neighbors ensures deterministic order.

sections.title: BFS on a graph
sections.explanation: Breadth-first search explores all neighbors at the current depth before moving deeper, using a `deque` as a FIFO queue and a `visited` set. BFS naturally finds the shortest path in unweighted graphs, making it the go-to for "minimum steps" and "nearest X" interview problems. Always mark a node visited when you enqueue it, not when you dequeue — this prevents duplicates.

exercise.prompt: Implement BFS starting from node 0 using a `deque`. Mark nodes visited when enqueuing. Print each node as you dequeue it (space-separated). The graph is `{0: [1, 2], 1: [0, 3], 2: [0, 4], 3: [1], 4: [2]}`.

validation.message: BFS from 0 visiting sorted neighbors should produce 0 1 2 3 4
Use deque with popleft for FIFO order and a visited set to avoid revisits

exercise.hint: Check `if neighbor not in visited`, then add to visited and append `neighbor` to the queue.

sections.title: Connected components
sections.explanation: Counting connected components means finding how many separate groups of nodes exist in an undirected graph. Loop through every node — if it hasn't been visited, run DFS (or BFS) from it and increment a counter. This O(V + E) pattern is a staple in interview problems like "number of islands" and "number of provinces."

exercise.prompt: Count the connected components in the graph `{0: [1], 1: [0, 2], 2: [1], 3: [4], 4: [3], 5: []}`. Loop through all nodes, run DFS on unvisited ones, and increment a counter each time. Print the count.

validation.message: Loop through nodes, run DFS on unvisited ones, and increment count

exercise.hint: Check `if node not in visited`, then call `dfs(graph, node, visited)` and add `1` to count.
